summary:
The Tang Dynasty (618–907 AD) is widely considered the golden age of Chinese civilization — a period of political unity, territorial expansion, cosmopolitan culture, economic prosperity, and extraordinary artistic and literary achievement that set the standard by which subsequent Chinese dynasties measured themselves. Founded by Li Yuan (Emperor Gaozu) after the brief reunifying [[event:2154|Sui Dynasty]], the Tang established its capital at Chang'an, which with a population of roughly one million people was the largest city in the world and one of the most cosmopolitan — home to Persian, Arab, Central Asian, Korean, Japanese, and Byzantine merchants, diplomats, and clergy.

The Tang's [[event:2152|golden age]] under Emperor Xuanzong (r. 712–756 AD) produced the greatest flowering of Chinese poetry — Li Bai and Du Fu, considered China's two greatest poets, wrote during this period — along with major advances in painting, sculpture, music, and Buddhist art. The [[event:2957|Empress Wu Zetian]] (r. 690–705 AD) was the only woman to rule China as emperor in her own name, achieving significant military and administrative successes. The [[event:2153|An Lushan Rebellion]] (755–763 AD) severely damaged the dynasty's power, triggering a partial recovery but permanent weakening that eventually led to the dynasty's collapse in 907 AD and the fragmented [[event:2157|Five Dynasties period]].

The Tang's cultural legacy extended far beyond China's borders: Tang artistic styles influenced Korea, Japan, and Central Asia; the imperial examination system was refined and expanded; Buddhism reached its greatest Chinese influence before the Huichang Suppression of 845 AD; and the Silk Road under Tang control enabled unprecedented commercial and cultural exchange across Eurasia.
